original_abstract: This article addresses a persistent challenge in tertiary English language education: the noticeable lack of qualitative progress in the written and oral performance of certain students. Research findings presented in this paper confirm a trend in which most learners reach a steady plateau—typically at the B2 level—upon entering university. In response, the authors examine the limitations of prevailing pedagogical methods and advocate for a paradigm shift toward approaches that foster self-confidence, creativity, and linguistic experimentation. The study explores whether such methods can effectively empower students to take greater responsibility for their own learning processes. Evaluation of these instructional changes, reflected in student feedback from the survey, suggests a positive impact on learner engagement and developmental growth.
humanized_abstract: Qualities of Failure: Addressing Stagnation in University ELP Programmes This paper is concerned with an on-going problem in postgraduate English Language teacher education. It is the problem of identifying, teaching and assessing students whose written and spoken language is developing in only qualitative terms. That is to say that in terms of the ELC Graded Examinations in Spoken English (GESE) and Certificates in English for International Citizens (CEIC) or the CEFR, the great majority of ELP students at university enter the plateau phase, at approximately B2 level. In order to tackle the problems that this stagnation presents, we aim in this paper to identify the weaknesses of conventional methodologies designed to foster the development of students in the plateau phase, and to explore the potential of more exploratory approaches to language teaching aimed at increasing students' confidence in their own learning and their willingness to take creative risks and to experiment in the use of the language in their study and in their own speech and writing. An ELP programme was implemented with new approaches derived from Post-Method Discourse research that lead to the empowerment of the students to be active participants in their learning process. Preliminary evidence obtained through the survey of students reflecting on their experience supports our claims.
